agent_output: {"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Bird and external domain expert Yao directly contradict each other on the fundamental domain model for multi-vendor marketplace orders. Bird defines an order as a single atomic entity with one status where partial fulfillment is invalid and one vendor failure cancels the entire order. Yao states the correct model is a composite order (basket) containing independent sub-orders per vendor, each with its own status and lifecycle, where a single vendor failure only cancels that vendor's sub-order. These are mutually exclusive domain models — they disagree on: (1) whether an order is atomic or composite, (2) whether partial fulfillment exists, and (3) the blast radius of a single vendor failure. This cannot be papered over; Shaq would build fundamentally different data models, state machines, and failure-handling logic depending on which model is correct.","agents_involved":["Bird","Yao (external domain expert)"],"routed_to":"Coach K","options":["Option A: Bird's atomic model — simpler to build, but punishes all vendors and the customer when one vendor fails. May be valid for low-vendor-count or tightly-coupled marketplaces.","Option B: Yao's composite/sub-order model — industry standard for marketplaces (Amazon, Shopify). More complex but isolates vendor failures. Payment is against the basket, fulfillment is per sub-order.","Option C: Reconvene Bird and Yao to determine if there is a bounded context distinction — e.g., the 'order' is atomic from a payment perspective but composite from a fulfillment perspective. This may resolve the apparent contradiction."],"recommendation":"Route back to Bird with Yao's input. Bird is Domain Authority and should either (a) rebut Yao's model with domain-specific reasoning for why atomic orders are correct in THIS marketplace, or (b) revise the domain model to incorporate sub-orders. Option C is most likely to yield the correct answer — the two models may not be fully incompatible if payment and fulfillment are separated into distinct bounded contexts. Additionally, MJ's architecture summary is still missing and should be blocked until this domain question is resolved."}],"confidence":{"level":25,"high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and material — these are mutually exclusive domain models that produce fundamentally different implementations","Payment is taken once in both models — that much is agreed"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which domain model is correct for this specific marketplace","Whether MJ's missing architecture work would have surfaced this same tension","Whether there are additional domain constraints not yet surfaced that would resolve the disagreement"],"assumptions":["Yao's domain note represents genuine marketplace domain expertise and is not a misunderstanding of the requirements","MJ has not submitted work through an alternate channel","Bird's analysis was produced without awareness of Yao's input"]}}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the situation. I need to synthesize Bird's domain analysis and Yao's domain expert note, and check if MJ has submitted anything.

Key observations:
1. Bird says: single order, single status, no partial fulfillment, one vendor failure cancels everything
2. Yao (domain expert) says: composite order with sub-orders per vendor, per-vendor lifecycle, single vendor failure only cancels that sub-order

These directly contradict each other on fundamental domain modeling decisions. This is a contradiction.

Also, MJ hasn't submitted an architecture summary — that's a missing agent output.


Now I'm realizing the escalation classification constraint: all escalations must have the same type. The core issue is that Bird's domain authority contradicts Yao's external expertise, and I can't move forward until this is resolved. MJ's missing submission is also blocking, but the contradiction between these two conflicting models is what I need to escalate first.

content.text: {"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Bird and external domain expert Yao directly contradict each other on the fundamental domain model for multi-vendor marketplace orders. Bird defines an order as a single atomic entity with one status where partial fulfillment is invalid and one vendor failure cancels the entire order. Yao states the correct model is a composite order (basket) containing independent sub-orders per vendor, each with its own status and lifecycle, where a single vendor failure only cancels that vendor's sub-order. These are mutually exclusive domain models — they disagree on: (1) whether an order is atomic or composite, (2) whether partial fulfillment exists, and (3) the blast radius of a single vendor failure. This cannot be papered over; Shaq would build fundamentally different data models, state machines, and failure-handling logic depending on which model is correct.","agents_involved":["Bird","Yao (external domain expert)"],"routed_to":"Coach K","options":["Option A: Bird's atomic model — simpler to build, but punishes all vendors and the customer when one vendor fails. May be valid for low-vendor-count or tightly-coupled marketplaces.","Option B: Yao's composite/sub-order model — industry standard for marketplaces (Amazon, Shopify). More complex but isolates vendor failures. Payment is against the basket, fulfillment is per sub-order.","Option C: Reconvene Bird and Yao to determine if there is a bounded context distinction — e.g., the 'order' is atomic from a payment perspective but composite from a fulfillment perspective. This may resolve the apparent contradiction."],"recommendation":"Route back to Bird with Yao's input. Bird is Domain Authority and should either (a) rebut Yao's model with domain-specific reasoning for why atomic orders are correct in THIS marketplace, or (b) revise the domain model to incorporate sub-orders. Option C is most likely to yield the correct answer — the two models may not be fully incompatible if payment and fulfillment are separated into distinct bounded contexts. Additionally, MJ's architecture summary is still missing and should be blocked until this domain question is resolved."}],"confidence":{"level":25,"high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and material — these are mutually exclusive domain models that produce fundamentally different implementations","Payment is taken once in both models — that much is agreed"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which domain model is correct for this specific marketplace","Whether MJ's missing architecture work would have surfaced this same tension","Whether there are additional domain constraints not yet surfaced that would resolve the disagreement"],"assumptions":["Yao's domain note represents genuine marketplace domain expertise and is not a misunderstanding of the requirements","MJ has not submitted work through an alternate channel","Bird's analysis was produced without awareness of Yao's input"]}}
